entry.narrative_markdown:
## Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, the Father of Patrick Flynn?

**Conclusion: Disproved.** The available evidence affirmatively refutes the claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary (born circa 1800), was the father of Patrick Flynn (born circa 1845, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania; died 12 March 1908). The claim originated in an unsourced FamilySearch family tree and was repeated by several derivative online trees, none citing a supporting record.

### Identifying the Candidate Father

The online tree describes the candidate father as Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, born approximately 1800, wife named Bridget. The Ballingarry (County Tipperary) Catholic parish burial register — an original source recording a contemporaneous entry by the officiating priest — records the burial of Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry on **14 November 1842**, with identifying details (townland, approximate age of about 42, wife Bridget) consistent with the man the online tree names.¹ On the basis of these corroborating identifiers, the person identified as I2 in this project is the Thomas Flynn the online tree's claim refers to.

### The Chronological Refutation

The burial record establishes that this Thomas Flynn died in November 1842 — nearly three years before Patrick Flynn was born. Two independent original sources corroborate Patrick's birth circa 1845:

- The **1850 U.S. federal census** for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, records Patrick Flynn, age 5, in the household of widow Bridget Flynn² — an age implying birth approximately 1845. Even allowing a census-age margin of one or two years, the birth falls no earlier than approximately 1843, still after Thomas's death. The birth-year information is of indeterminate quality (informant unknown, possibly a neighbor or household member), and the evidence is indirect (age rather than a stated birth date), but the record is original and the conclusion is consistent across its range.
- The **1908 Pennsylvania death certificate** for Patrick Flynn records his age at death as approximately 63, independently implying birth circa 1845.³ This information is secondary (reported by son-in-law James Brown, who was not present at Patrick's birth), but it independently corroborates the 1850 census figure.

A man buried in November 1842 cannot have fathered a child born in 1845. The chronological impossibility is absolute. No record has been found supporting the online tree's assertion; the one record that identifies the specific Thomas Flynn named in that tree proves he was already dead before Patrick's birth.

### Scope and Limitations

The three record sets searched (1850 census, Ballingarry parish burial register, 1908 death certificate) are sufficient to disprove this specific parentage claim. Research is not exhaustive for the broader question of Patrick Flynn's actual parentage: no alternative father has been identified, census records for 1860–1900, naturalization papers, and church baptismal records for Pennsylvania have not been searched. Those searches bear on the open question of who Patrick's father was — not on whether Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was that father, which is disproved on the current evidence. Previously unknown evidence could in theory alter this conclusion, but no plausible avenue exists by which a burial recorded in November 1842 could be error by three or more years in a way that would resurrect the claim.

---

¹ Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:BALL1842), accessed 4 May 2026.

² 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M1850W), accessed 2 May 2026.

³ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 6 May 2026.

added.narrative_markdown:
## Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, the Father of Patrick Flynn?

**Conclusion: Disproved.** The available evidence affirmatively refutes the claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary (born circa 1800), was the father of Patrick Flynn (born circa 1845, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania; died 12 March 1908). The claim originated in an unsourced FamilySearch family tree and was repeated by several derivative online trees, none citing a supporting record.

### Identifying the Candidate Father

The online tree describes the candidate father as Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, born approximately 1800, wife named Bridget. The Ballingarry (County Tipperary) Catholic parish burial register — an original source recording a contemporaneous entry by the officiating priest — records the burial of Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry on **14 November 1842**, with identifying details (townland, approximate age of about 42, wife Bridget) consistent with the man the online tree names.¹ On the basis of these corroborating identifiers, the person identified as I2 in this project is the Thomas Flynn the online tree's claim refers to.

### The Chronological Refutation

The burial record establishes that this Thomas Flynn died in November 1842 — nearly three years before Patrick Flynn was born. Two independent original sources corroborate Patrick's birth circa 1845:

- The **1850 U.S. federal census** for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, records Patrick Flynn, age 5, in the household of widow Bridget Flynn² — an age implying birth approximately 1845. Even allowing a census-age margin of one or two years, the birth falls no earlier than approximately 1843, still after Thomas's death. The birth-year information is of indeterminate quality (informant unknown, possibly a neighbor or household member), and the evidence is indirect (age rather than a stated birth date), but the record is original and the conclusion is consistent across its range.
- The **1908 Pennsylvania death certificate** for Patrick Flynn records his age at death as approximately 63, independently implying birth circa 1845.³ This information is secondary (reported by son-in-law James Brown, who was not present at Patrick's birth), but it independently corroborates the 1850 census figure.

A man buried in November 1842 cannot have fathered a child born in 1845. The chronological impossibility is absolute. No record has been found supporting the online tree's assertion; the one record that identifies the specific Thomas Flynn named in that tree proves he was already dead before Patrick's birth.

### Scope and Limitations

The three record sets searched (1850 census, Ballingarry parish burial register, 1908 death certificate) are sufficient to disprove this specific parentage claim. Research is not exhaustive for the broader question of Patrick Flynn's actual parentage: no alternative father has been identified, census records for 1860–1900, naturalization papers, and church baptismal records for Pennsylvania have not been searched. Those searches bear on the open question of who Patrick's father was — not on whether Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was that father, which is disproved on the current evidence. Previously unknown evidence could in theory alter this conclusion, but no plausible avenue exists by which a burial recorded in November 1842 could be error by three or more years in a way that would resurrect the claim.

---

¹ Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:BALL1842), accessed 4 May 2026.

² 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M1850W), accessed 2 May 2026.

³ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 6 May 2026.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry Was Not Patrick Flynn's Father: A Proof Summary

## Research Question

Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary (born about 1800), the father of Patrick Flynn, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania? The question originates from an unsourced entry in the FamilySearch Family Tree, copied by several online trees without any underlying documentation.

## Establishing Patrick Flynn's Birth Year

Two independent original sources place Patrick Flynn's birth at approximately 1845. The 1850 United States Federal Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, records Patrick Flynn as age 5 in the household of widow Bridget Flynn, implying birth about 1845 in Ireland.[1] The informant for this census entry is unidentified — most likely a household member or neighbor — making the information quality indeterminate; nonetheless, the age-derived birth year is direct evidence for when Patrick was born. The Pennsylvania death certificate for Patrick Flynn (certificate no. 4521, issued 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County) records his age at death as approximately 63, independently calculating to a birth year of about 1845.[2] The informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law — a family member not present at the birth — so the birth-year information on the certificate is secondary; it is nonetheless indirect evidence that corroborates the census figure. Both sources are original documents; together, they establish that Patrick was born no earlier than about 1843.

## Identifying Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry in the Burial Record

The Ballingarry (County Tipperary) Catholic parish burial register records the burial of Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry on 14 November 1842.[3] The entry was recorded by the officiating priest contemporaneously, making this an original source carrying primary information — the highest-quality classification available. The identifying details in the entry — townland of Ballingarry, age approximately 42, and wife named Bridget — correspond precisely to the Thomas Flynn described in the online family tree as Patrick's father. This correspondence establishes that the burial record documents the same individual the online tree claims as Patrick's father.

## Analysis and Conclusion

The online tree's claim is chronologically impossible. Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was in the ground by 14 November 1842. Patrick Flynn was born approximately 1845 — nearly three years after Thomas's burial. A man who died in 1842 cannot have fathered a child born in 1845. The parentage claim is not merely unsupported; it is affirmatively refuted by primary evidence in a contemporaneous original record.

No evidence supporting the claim was found in any records searched. The claim appears to originate as an unsourced assertion in an online family tree and to have propagated by copying, with no underlying documentary basis ever identified.

**The claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, was the father of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845) is disproved.** The father of Patrick Flynn remains unidentified on current evidence.

## Citations

[1] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

[2] Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 6 May 2026.

[3] Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 4 May 2026.

The plan targeted the Thomas Flynn named in the online tree and Patrick Flynn's birth evidence. Further record types — Griffith's Valuation, Tithe Applotments, emigration records, or Ballingarry baptismal registers for Patrick himself — were not exhausted, and research has not been formally declared exhaustive. However, the refuting evidence is conclusive on its own terms: a man buried in 1842 cannot have fathered a child born approximately 1845, and no additional searches could overcome that chronological impossibility.

Two independent original sources place Patrick Flynn's birth at approximately 1845. The 1850 United States Federal Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, records Patrick Flynn as age 5 in the household of widow Bridget Flynn, implying birth about 1845 in Ireland.[1] The informant for this census entry is unidentified — most likely a household member or neighbor — making the information quality indeterminate; nonetheless, the age-derived birth year is direct evidence for when Patrick was born. The Pennsylvania death certificate for Patrick Flynn (certificate no. 4521, issued 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County) records his age at death as approximately 63, independently calculating to a birth year of about 1845.[2] The informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law — a family member not present at the birth — so the birth-year information on the certificate is secondary; it is nonetheless indirect evidence that corroborates the census figure. Both sources are original documents; together, they establish that Patrick was born no earlier than about 1843.

## Identifying Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry in the Burial Record

The Ballingarry (County Tipperary) Catholic parish burial register records the burial of Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry on 14 November 1842.[3] The entry was recorded by the officiating priest contemporaneously, making this an original source carrying primary information — the highest-quality classification available. The identifying details in the entry — townland of Ballingarry, age approximately 42, and wife named Bridget — correspond precisely to the Thomas Flynn described in the online family tree as Patrick's father. This correspondence establishes that the burial record documents the same individual the online tree claims as Patrick's father.

## Analysis and Conclusion

The online tree's claim is chronologically impossible. Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was in the ground by 14 November 1842. Patrick Flynn was born approximately 1845 — nearly three years after Thomas's burial. A man who died in 1842 cannot have fathered a child born in 1845. The parentage claim is not merely unsupported; it is affirmatively refuted by primary evidence in a contemporaneous original record.

No evidence supporting the claim was found in any records searched. The claim appears to originate as an unsourced assertion in an online family tree and to have propagated by copying, with no underlying documentary basis ever identified.

**The claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, was the father of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845) is disproved.** The father of Patrick Flynn remains unidentified on current evidence.

## Citations

[1] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

[2] Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 6 May 2026.

[3] Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 4 May 2026.

output.text_response:
Now I have everything I need. Let me assess ps_001 against the five GPS components.

---

## GPS Assessment of ps_001 — Parentage of Patrick Flynn (q_001)

**Proof summary:** `ps_001` | Tier: **Probable** | Form: **Summary**
**Evidence cited:** a_004 (1850 census, household position), a_010 (1860 census, household position), a_013 (death certificate, father named)
**Conflict cited:** c_001 (birthplace conflict — Ireland vs. Pennsylvania)

This is a **review-only assessment**; no new or updated proof summary is written.

---

### GPS Component 1 — Reasonably Exhaustive Research
**Status: Incomplete — correctly flagged and handled at Probable tier ✅**

ps_001 openly acknowledges three repositories for the 1850 census (FamilySearch, Ancestry, MyHeritage), the 1860 census (FamilySearch), and the death certificate (FamilySearch). The exhaustive_search_summary and the Assessment paragraph of the narrative both name the unsearched record sets: 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses; probate (in progress). The question's `exhaustive_declaration` is `declared: false`, which is consistent with the narrative's honesty.

This is the correct way to handle an incomplete search at the Probable tier: the narrative tells the reader exactly what is missing and why it keeps the conclusion below Proved. No GPS violation — the component is incomplete, the tier reflects that incompleteness, and the narrative documents it.

---

### GPS Component 2 — Complete, Accurate Citations
**Status: Met for the evidence ps_001 cites ✅**

All three cited sources carry full, well-formed narrative citations:

| Source | Citation elements present |
|--------|--------------------------|
| 1850 census | NARA microfilm M432/roll 810, FamilySearch.org, dwelling/family locator, access date |
| 1860 census | NARA microfilm M653/roll 1141, FamilySearch.org, dwelling/family locator, access date |
| Death cert | Cert no. 4521, PA Dept. of Health, PA State Archives, FamilySearch.org, access date |

Each citation is traceable and replicable. This component is met as scoped to what ps_001 actually cites.

---

### GPS Component 3 — Analysis and Correlation
**Status: Met ✅**

ps_001 demonstrates all three layers of classification for each cited source:

- **Source type:** 1850 and 1860 censuses labeled "Original Source"; death certificate labeled "Original Source" — correct.
- **Information quality:** Census informants identified as "indeterminate but likely a household member"; death certificate informant (James Brown, son-in-law) identified as **secondary** for parentage facts — correct and appropriately reasoned.
- **Evidence type:** Census household position evidence correctly labeled **indirect** (1850/1860 censuses did not record relationships; relationship column introduced in 1880). Death certificate correctly labeled **direct** evidence naming the father.

Correlation across sources is explicit: all three independently converge on Thomas Flynn as father, despite differing informant quality. The narrative organizes by evidential significance, not research chronology — a sign of well-structured GPS reasoning.

One minor observation: the narrative calls the informant for the death certificate's parentage entry "secondary" but does not name James Brown's relationship to the knowledge — i.e., it doesn't articulate *why* a son-in-law is secondary (he was not present at Patrick's birth; he knew Patrick only as an adult). The `informant_bias_notes` in a_013 covers this, but the narrative Summary doesn't surface that reasoning explicitly. This is a small narrative-polish gap, not a GPS failure.

---

### GPS Component 4 — Resolution of Conflicting Evidence
**Status: Met ✅**

c_001 (birthplace: Ireland vs. Pennsylvania) is the only conflict in the project, and ps_001 handles it fully:

1. **Acknowledged explicitly** — yes, dedicated "Conflict Resolution" section ✓
2. **Reliability analyzed** — contemporary census vs. 63-years-later death certificate; closer informants vs. secondary ✓
3. **Preferred version stated** — Ireland accepted ✓
4. **Explanation for the rejected version** — son-in-law may have confused residence with birthplace, or lacked knowledge of Patrick's Ireland birth ✓

All four resolution steps are satisfied. Note that c_001 concerns birthplace, not parentage — it does not touch the parentage conclusion directly — but it *is* the only conflict involving cited assertions (a_002, a_009, a_012), so addressing it is both appropriate and required to handle the death certificate evidence honestly.

---

### GPS Component 5 — Soundly Reasoned, Coherently Written Conclusion
**Status: Met ✅**

| Criterion | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|
| Research question identifiable | ✅ Stated in the sub-heading; implied in the conclusion sentence |
| Answer clearly stated | ✅ "Patrick Flynn is **Probably** the son of Thomas Flynn" |
| Phrasing matches tier | ✅ Conditional ("Probably," "supports") — appropriate for Probable; no Proved-tier definitives used |
| Organized logically | ✅ Evidential significance order, not research chronology |
| Explains *why* Probable not Proved | ✅ Three explicit reasons given in the Assessment paragraph |
| Acknowledges limitations | ✅ Unsearched records named; probate gap flagged |
| Self-contained | ✅ Readable without the JSON; citations embedded |

The form choice (Summary) is appropriate: multiple sources require correlation, and the weight is clearly one direction, but indirect census evidence and a secondary death-certificate informant require the reader to see the reasoning. An Argument is not yet warranted — there are no competing candidates, no significant parentage conflict (only the resolved birthplace conflict), and no indirect-only evidence base.

---

### Overall Verdict on ps_001

**The proof summary is well-constructed and defensible at the Probable tier.** All five GPS components are either met or — where incomplete (Component 1) — correctly acknowledged and reflected in the tier. The tier selection is accurate. The form selection is accurate. The narrative phrasing is consistent with the tier.

---

### Evidence Gaps for Future Rounds (Not ps_001 Defects)

The following sources exist in `research.json` but have no assertions extracted for q_001. They are **not** failures of the current proof — ps_001 can only cite what has been extracted. They are candidates for the next research round.

#### Tier-advancing gaps (high priority)

**`src_005` — St. Mary's Catholic Church baptism, 1845 (no q_001 assertions extracted)**
A baptismal register is an original source; the priest is a contemporary official recorder; the parents' names were typically supplied by the family at the time of baptism, making parentage information **primary** and **direct**. If this record names Thomas Flynn (and possibly a wife) as Patrick's parents, it would be the strongest single piece of direct evidence in the project — original + primary + direct. Combined with existing evidence and a completed census search, this could advance the tier to **Proved**. Citation is flagged as a rough working draft (missing volume/page locator and repository chain) — that should be corrected on extraction.

**`src_006` — Schuylkill County will of Thomas Flynn, 1881 (no q_001 assertions extracted)**
A will naming Patrick as "my son" would be an original source, with Thomas Flynn himself as the informant — making it **primary** information and **direct** evidence of parentage. The plan item `pli_006` has this search in progress. If found and extracted, this combined with the death certificate and census evidence would substantially strengthen the case. The working citation is rough (creator listed as "courthouse" not the court; missing Will Book volume and page) — needs correction.

Together, src_005 and src_006, if they produce the expected assertions, **would likely advance the tier to Proved** once research is declared exhaustive and remaining censuses are searched.

#### Corroborating gaps (lower priority, likely would not alone change the tier)

| Source | Nature | Likely impact |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| `src_007` — Mary Elizabeth Flynn birth cert, 1905 | Probably names Patrick as father — helps confirm Patrick's identity in later records, not his own parentage | No tier change on q_001 |
| `src_008` — Deed, Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn, 1881 | Father-to-son transfer is circumstantially suggestive; indirect evidence of relationship | Corroborating, not tier-changing alone |
| `src_009` — Patrick Flynn obituary, 1908 | May name parents or surviving relatives | Authored/secondary source; corroborating at best |

There are also four assertions tied to q_001 that ps_001 does not cite (a_005, a_008, a_009, a_012). All come from sources already cited in the proof (src_001, src_003, src_004). They add corroborating detail (Thomas Flynn's name as head, Patrick's consistent age in 1860, the birthplace discrepancy already handled in c_001) but would not change the tier — they're supplementary to what ps_001 already addresses.

---

### Recommended Next Step

The current Probable conclusion is solid. To advance it:

1. **Extract assertions from src_005 and src_006** — the baptism and will are the highest-leverage next items.
2. **Search remaining census years** (1870, 1880, 1900) per the active plan.
3. Once those are done, run **`research-exhaustiveness`** to evaluate whether q_001 can be declared exhaustive, then **`question-selection`** to decide whether to revise the proof or move to the next question.

output.text_response:
Good question — let me work through the GPS information-quality analysis carefully for **a_013** (Father: Thomas Flynn, from src_004).**"Secondary" is the correct classification for a_013.** Here's the full reasoning:

---

### The three-tier GPS framework applied to this fact

| Layer | Classification | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** (src_004) | Original | Death certificate created at time of event by a government office |
| **Information** (a_013) | **Secondary** ✓ | Informant lacked firsthand knowledge of the fact |
| **Evidence** | Direct | The record explicitly names "Thomas Flynn" as father |

---

### Why the information is definitively secondary — not primary or indeterminate

**Primary** would require firsthand knowledge. For Patrick's parentage, that means the informant witnessed or was a party to the parent-child relationship in a direct sense — essentially, the informant would need to be Patrick himself, or perhaps the mother. James Brown, as son-in-law, cannot possibly have firsthand knowledge of who fathered Patrick Flynn. He was not present at Patrick's birth (~1845) and is reporting what he was told, whether by his wife (Patrick's daughter), by Patrick himself during his lifetime, or by family tradition.

Even if Brown personally knew Thomas Flynn (plausible — Thomas died in 1881, so Brown may have met him), *knowing* a person does not give you firsthand knowledge of *that person's relationship* to your father-in-law.

**Indeterminate** is used when you genuinely cannot determine whether an informant had firsthand or secondhand knowledge. This case is not ambiguous — a son-in-law reporting his father-in-law's parentage is structurally incapable of having firsthand knowledge of that fact. We don't need to guess.

---

### The common confusion here

The death certificate is an **original source**, and for the *death facts* (a_011), the informant is the attending physician — a witness — making that information **primary**. But the same document contains **secondary information** for parentage facts, because a different informant (Brown) supplied those facts from secondhand knowledge. Source type and information quality are evaluated independently for each fact within a document.

**Bottom line:** The "secondary" classification on a_013 is correct and well-reasoned. The `informant_proximity` of `family_not_present` reinforces it — Brown was family by marriage but not present at, or a witness to, Patrick's parentage. No change needed.

aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified and articulated the `not_proved` tier based on equally-weighted, mutually irresolvable evidence for two candidate fathers. All factual claims are supported by the input state: two 1850 census households (Thomas and Michael Flynn), the 1860 boarder record showing Patrick separated from either household, the 1908 death certificate with blank father field, and the negative baptism search result. The exhaustive search summary accurately describes the repositories and record types examined.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: (1) selected the `not_proved` tier as directed; (2) presented both candidate households (Thomas and Michael Flynn) with equal weight; (3) incorporated all negative evidence—the 1860 boarder record showing no connection, the blank death certificate father field, and the nil baptism search; (4) explained why each candidate cannot be confirmed; (5) declared the research exhaustive; (6) did not update tree.gedcomx.json (tier below probable, no relationship write). All 12 assertions (a_001–a_012) are listed in supporting_assertion_ids. No major portions of the required output were omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Two MCP calls were made. First call appended a proof_summary entry with correct section name ('proof_summaries'), operation ('append'), and a properly-structured entry object including question_id (q_001), tier (not_proved), vehicle (argument), all 12 supporting_assertion_ids, empty resolved_conflict_ids array (correct—no conflicts), exhaustive_search_summary, and narrative_markdown. Second call updated the project section with fields object (empty, which is acceptable for a touch). Both calls used correct projectPath and the mcp__genealogy__research_append tool. Neither call returned a fixture_not_found error. Arguments match the expected shape and content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tier justification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The `not_proved` tier is fully justified. The narrative explicitly explains why this tier and not higher or lower: two candidates (Thomas and Michael Flynn) carry identical evidential weight from the same record type (1850 census); no record bridges either to the adult subject; the death certificate informant had no knowledge; and the most probative record type (baptism) is absent. The skill states clearly that 'The evidence does not favor either Thomas Flynn or Michael Flynn as Patrick's father. Neither hypothesis can be confirmed; neither can be eliminated.' This directly addresses what would be needed for a higher tier (a bridging record or a probative source naming the father directly). The exhaustive search declaration supports the tier.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The narrative is fully self-contained and publication-ready. Every assertion is described in prose: both 1850 census candidates (age, dwelling, name, source information, quality classification); the 1860 boarder record (why it doesn't connect); the 1908 death certificate (original, informant identity, secondary status, negative result on father field); and the baptism search (nil result). Inline citations are present throughout (e.g., 'NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026'). Conflict resolution rationale is included ('Two equally credible candidates cannot be distinguished on present evidence'). The tier is explicitly declared in the Conclusion section. A reader with no access to research.json can fully understand the case and the reasoning.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 12 relevant assertions (a_001–a_012) are cited in the new narrative. Positive evidence is presented: both 1850 census households, their characteristics, and why each is plausible. Negative evidence is fully incorporated: the 1860 boarder record (no connection to either household), the death certificate father field blank (confirming loss of knowledge within the family), and the baptism search nil result. The narrative does not selectively present only supporting evidence; instead it builds a complete evidentiary picture explaining why neither candidate can be distinguished or confirmed. No inconvenient sources are omitted; all four record types examined (two 1850 census searches, 1860 census, 1908 death cert, baptism registers) are discussed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The Proof Argument form is the correct vehicle for this evidence shape. The case involves competing candidates (Thomas vs. Michael Flynn), only indirect evidence (inferred household co-residence from 1850 census), and attempted-but-failed elimination (the 1860 and death certificate records neither confirm nor exclude either candidate). The narrative's structure follows the Argument form: it presents both candidates with equal weight, explains their identical evidential basis, lays out the failed bridging evidence, addresses the negative sources, synthesizes all findings, and concludes that the answer cannot be determined. A Statement would be inappropriate (no direct evidence settles it); a Summary would misrepresent the complexity (two equally credible candidates, not one leaning clearly ahead). The reader can follow the full reasoning chain and understand why disambiguation is impossible.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tree encoding
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: N/A—the concluded tier is `not_proved`, which is below the `probable` threshold. Per the skill's invariant, no tree relationship write is expected at this tier. This dimension applies only at `probable`+ tiers.

## Preconditions Gate

**Assertions linked to q_001:** a_001 through a_012 (all 12).

**Classification check:**
All 12 carry reasoned `information_quality` + `evidence_type` values with `informant`/`informant_proximity` analysis — not defaults. ✓

**Person_evidence check (identity):**
- I1 (Patrick, subject): identity assertions via pe_001 (a_001 name), pe_002 (a_005 name), pe_005 (a_009 name) ✓
- I2 (Thomas Flynn, candidate father A): identity via pe_003 (a_004 name) ✓
- I3 (Michael Flynn, candidate father B): identity via pe_004 (a_008 name) ✓

*Advisory (fact/negative assertions for already-identified persons, not blockers):* a_002, a_003, a_006, a_007, a_010, a_012 — all link to persons already resolved above.

**Conflicts:** none. ✓

**Gate passes.** Proceeding.

---

**Tier:** `not_proved` — Two candidates (Thomas Flynn, Michael Flynn) carry identical evidential weight; no record bridges either to the adult subject; the death certificate informant reported the father as unknown; no baptism survived. The evidence cannot lean toward either candidate, let alone prove one. Research exhaustive.

**Form:** `argument` — Competing candidates with only indirect evidence; a reader would ask "why can't you choose?"Proof summary written. Now stamping the project updated (no status change — question still open).---

**Tier: `not_proved`** — The evidence cannot lean toward either candidate. Both the Thomas Flynn (dwelling 84) and Michael Flynn (dwelling 210) hypotheses rest on identical indirect evidence from an original source with indeterminate information quality; no record distinguishes one from the other. Research is exhaustive and the death certificate informant had no knowledge of the father's name. GPS components 1 (exhaustive), 2 (citations), 3 (analysis), and 5 (written argument) are met — but component 5 can only conclude *not proved*, because component 4 (conflict resolution) is moot and there is simply no evidence to weigh in favor of either candidate.

**What was written:**
- **ps_001** — proof argument for q_001 ("Who was Patrick's father?"), tier `not_proved`
- No tree relationship written (tier < probable; no ParentChild link is appropriate)
- Project `updated` stamped

The full argument is persisted in `ps_001` (`narrative_markdown`). It walks through:
- Candidate A (Thomas Flynn, 1850 dwelling 84) — original / indeterminate / indirect
- Candidate B (Michael Flynn, 1850 dwelling 210) — original / indeterminate / indirect
- 1860 boarder record — why it doesn't connect to either household
- 1908 death certificate — original / secondary / negative (son-in-law reported father "unknown")
- Catholic register nil result

**Next step:** Run **question-selection** — this is the only question in the project, and its answer is *not proved*, so question-selection should assess whether any further avenue exists (e.g., a FAN pivot, DNA evidence, or an unindexed record repository) or formally close the project.

entry.exhaustive_search_summary: Searched the 1850 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County (FamilySearch, NARA M432) for Patrick Flynn as a child; the 1860 U.S. Census (FamilySearch, NARA M653) for Patrick as a young adult; the 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate (FamilySearch, Pennsylvania State Archives); and Catholic parish registers for Schuylkill County and the County Tipperary emigrant cohort via FamilySearch and the National Library of Ireland (1843–1850). These record types represent the full universe of repositories likely to contain parentage information for an Irish immigrant coal laborer in mid-nineteenth-century Schuylkill County. The death certificate informant (son-in-law James Brown) reported the father as unknown. No baptism record was located. Research is declared exhaustive on q_001.
entry.narrative_markdown:
## Patrick Flynn's Father: Not Proved

**Research question:** Who was the father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

**Conclusion:** The father of Patrick Flynn cannot be determined on the evidence presently available. Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill County, dwelling 84, 1850 census) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill County, dwelling 210, 1850 census) — present equally weighted, mutually irresolvable indirect evidence. No record connects adult Patrick to either candidate household, and the available evidence does not favor either candidate. Research has been declared exhaustive on this question (q_001).

---

### Evidence Assessed

**1850 U.S. Census — Candidate Household A (Thomas Flynn)**

The 1850 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County records a child named Patrick Flynn, age 5, born in Ireland, residing in the household of Thomas Flynn (head), dwelling 84, family 91 (1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026). Age 5 in 1850 implies a birth year of approximately 1845, consistent with the subject's known birth date. This source is *original*. The information quality is *indeterminate* — the informant is unknown, most likely a household member or neighbor responding to the enumerator. The evidence is *indirect*: the 1850 census did not record household relationships, so the child's position as a co-resident of a same-surnamed adult is an inference, not a stated father–child tie. This record supports the hypothesis that Thomas Flynn was the subject's father, but the inference rests entirely on the unconfirmed assumption that this particular child is the subject.

**1850 U.S. Census — Candidate Household B (Michael Flynn)**

The same 1850 Schuylkill County census search returned a second household with a child Patrick Flynn, age 6, born in Ireland, residing with Michael Flynn (head), dwelling 210, family 233 (1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, dwelling 210, family 233, Michael Flynn household; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026). Age 6 in 1850 implies birth approximately 1844 — a one-year discrepancy from the subject's estimated birth year, within the normal margin of census age reporting. This source is *original*. The information quality is again *indeterminate* for the same reasons. The evidence is *indirect* for the same structural reason: household co-residence is inferential, not stated. This record supports the hypothesis that Michael Flynn was the subject's father, on the same speculative basis as candidate A.

**Why the two candidates are indistinguishable**

Both candidate households are drawn from the same census year, the same county, and the same original record type. Both produce an Irish-born child named Patrick Flynn of matching age. Both provide only indirect evidence via inferred household relationship. The informants for both are unknown. Neither household yields any additional identifying detail — a wife's name, a townland of origin, an occupation — that could be compared against later records of the adult subject. The evidential weight assigned to each candidate is identical.

**1860 U.S. Census — Adult Patrick (Negative Bridging Evidence)**

The 1860 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County (original record, dwelling 305, Owen Donnelly household; NARA microfilm M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026) places Patrick Flynn, age 15, coal laborer, born Ireland, as a boarder with an entirely unrelated family. This source is *original*. His age and birthplace are consistent with the subject. The evidence is *indirect* and serves primarily as negative bridging evidence: neither Thomas nor Michael Flynn appears in or near the Donnelly household. If Patrick had remained with his father into early adulthood, some proximity might be expected; instead, he boards independently. This is suggestive of separation from his family of origin — possibly orphanhood, economic necessity, or migration — but it is not probative in either direction, as such separation was common among Irish immigrant laborers. This record does not link adult Patrick to either candidate household and cannot advance the question of paternity.

**1908 Pennsylvania Death Certificate — Negative Evidence for the Father**

The 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate for Patrick Flynn (original record, cert. no. 4521; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 5 May 2026) is unambiguously for the subject — his death date, location, and subsequent family genealogy confirm identity. This source is *original*. The attending physician provided *primary*, *direct* evidence of the death event itself (12 March 1908, Schuylkill County). The informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law — a person with family proximity but lacking firsthand knowledge of Patrick's parentage; his information on the father is accordingly *secondary*. The evidence on the father is *negative*: Brown reported the father as "unknown," and the father field was left blank. This is the record type most likely to resolve the question directly; instead it confirms that Patrick's paternal identity was already lost within his own family by 1908. It neither confirms nor excludes either Thomas or Michael Flynn.

**Catholic Parish Registers — Nil Result**

Catholic parish registers for Schuylkill County and the County Tipperary emigrant cohort (1843–1850) were searched via FamilySearch and the National Library of Ireland (NLI), accessed 7 May 2026. No baptism for a Patrick Flynn naming a father was located. Pennsylvania Catholic registers from this period are incomplete; the absence of a baptism record does not exclude either candidate, but it leaves the most probative record type — a baptism that would have named the father directly — as a nil result.

---

### Synthesis

Four independent record types were examined, and each contributes the following:

- The two 1850 census records produce two equally plausible candidate fathers with identical evidential weight — neither is stronger than the other.
- The 1860 census establishes the adult subject's identity and location but provides no connection to either 1850 household.
- The 1908 death certificate confirms the subject but provides only negative evidence for the father: the family had lost knowledge of the paternal line.
- The Catholic register search produced a nil result, leaving the most probative record type absent.

No additional record repositories exist that could plausibly resolve this question for an Irish immigrant laborer in mid-nineteenth-century Schuylkill County. Research is declared exhaustive (declaration on file, q_001).

The evidence does not favor either Thomas Flynn or Michael Flynn as Patrick's father. Neither hypothesis can be confirmed; neither can be eliminated. Two equally credible candidates cannot be distinguished on present evidence.

---

### Conclusion

**The father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) is not proved.** Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill Co., dwelling 84, 1850) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill Co., dwelling 210, 1850) — each hold equally weighted indirect evidence based on inferred household co-residence in the 1850 census. No record bridges either childhood household to the adult subject. The subject's own family, as represented by his son-in-law in 1908, had no knowledge of his father's name. No baptism record survives to supply direct evidence. Under the Genealogical Proof Standard, this conclusion is *not proved*: the evidence is insufficient to lean toward either candidate.

This conclusion remains open to revision if a previously unindexed record comes to light — particularly a Catholic baptism, a family Bible record, or a confirmation record from this period — that names Patrick Flynn's father. No such record is presently known to exist.

Catholic parish registers for Schuylkill County and the County Tipperary emigrant cohort (1843–1850) were searched via FamilySearch and the National Library of Ireland (NLI), accessed 7 May 2026. No baptism for a Patrick Flynn naming a father was located. Pennsylvania Catholic registers from this period are incomplete; the absence of a baptism record does not exclude either candidate, but it leaves the most probative record type — a baptism that would have named the father directly — as a nil result.

---

### Synthesis

Four independent record types were examined, and each contributes the following:

- The two 1850 census records produce two equally plausible candidate fathers with identical evidential weight — neither is stronger than the other.
- The 1860 census establishes the adult subject's identity and location but provides no connection to either 1850 household.
- The 1908 death certificate confirms the subject but provides only negative evidence for the father: the family had lost knowledge of the paternal line.
- The Catholic register search produced a nil result, leaving the most probative record type absent.

No additional record repositories exist that could plausibly resolve this question for an Irish immigrant laborer in mid-nineteenth-century Schuylkill County. Research is declared exhaustive (declaration on file, q_001).

The evidence does not favor either Thomas Flynn or Michael Flynn as Patrick's father. Neither hypothesis can be confirmed; neither can be eliminated. Two equally credible candidates cannot be distinguished on present evidence.

---

### Conclusion

**The father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) is not proved.** Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill Co., dwelling 84, 1850) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill Co., dwelling 210, 1850) — each hold equally weighted indirect evidence based on inferred household co-residence in the 1850 census. No record bridges either childhood household to the adult subject. The subject's own family, as represented by his son-in-law in 1908, had no knowledge of his father's name. No baptism record survives to supply direct evidence. Under the Genealogical Proof Standard, this conclusion is *not proved*: the evidence is insufficient to lean toward either candidate.

This conclusion remains open to revision if a previously unindexed record comes to light — particularly a Catholic baptism, a family Bible record, or a confirmation record from this period — that names Patrick Flynn's father. No such record is presently known to exist.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Possible Parentage of Patrick Flynn: A Proof Summary

**Research Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, probably in Ireland; died 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

## Evidence

A single source bears on this question. The 1850 U.S. Federal Census, population schedule for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, dwelling 84, family 91, lists a male child named "Patrick Flynn," age 5, born in Ireland, in the household headed by Thomas Flynn, age approximately 32, also born in Ireland.[1] The source is an original record — the manuscript population schedule created by the enumerator at the time of enumeration.

The information quality for Patrick's name, age, and birthplace is indeterminate. The 1850 census does not identify the person who supplied information to the enumerator; a household member such as Thomas Flynn is the most plausible informant, but enumerators also gathered information from neighbors when residents were unavailable, and the record provides no way to determine who answered. Accordingly, the information cannot be classified as primary (from a witness to the birth) or secondary (from a retrospective account by a known informant).

The evidence bearing on Patrick Flynn's parentage is indirect. The 1850 census does not record household relationships. The inference of a parent-child connection rests on three observations: Patrick's position immediately below the household head, his shared surname, and an approximate 27-year age gap — all consistent with a father-child relationship, but equally consistent with a nephew, grandson, ward, or unrelated boarder of the same surname. This household-structure inference is the sole evidentiary basis for the parentage hypothesis; no informant reported a relationship, and the census itself states nothing about relationships.

## Research Gaps and Exhaustiveness

Research on this question is not exhaustive. Two high-priority record types identified in the research plan remain unsearched:

1. **1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania** — would reveal whether Patrick was still living in Thomas Flynn's household at approximately age 15, substantially corroborating the candidate relationship.
2. **1908 Pennsylvania death certificate for Patrick Flynn** — Pennsylvania death certificates from this period commonly name the decedent's parents; if found and complete, it would provide direct evidence of parentage.

Until these records are examined, the parentage hypothesis rests on a single indirect inference from one census and cannot reach probable or proved.

## Conclusion

Based on currently available evidence, it is possible that Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, Ireland; died 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) was a son of Thomas Flynn, who headed the Schuylkill County household in which Patrick was enumerated at age 5 in 1850.[1] The conclusion is rated possible — not probable — because the sole supporting evidence is a single indirect inference from household co-residence in a census that does not state relationships, and because research is not exhaustive. Examination of the 1860 census and Patrick's 1908 death certificate, as planned, could advance this conclusion to probable or proved, or could introduce competing evidence requiring resolution.

---

[1] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Proof Summary: The Parentage of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)

Patrick Flynn, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, is the son of Thomas Flynn. This conclusion rests on five independent sources — two of which are original records with primary information on the parent-child relationship.

### Primary Evidence

The 12 March 1845 Catholic baptismal register of St. Mary's parish, County Tipperary, Ireland, records the baptism of Patricius Flynn, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan, witnessed and recorded by the officiating priest, Rev. Michael O'Connor.¹ Both parents were present at the ceremony; the priest had no incentive to misrecord. This record is an original source carrying primary information and constitutes direct evidence of the parent-child relationship. The Flynn family Bible (Dublin imprint, 1840), held privately by a Flynn descendant, records Patrick Flynn, born 1845, as the son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan in a single consistent contemporaneous hand.² The Bible imprint (1840) predates the birth entries, supporting contemporaneous recording rather than later transcription. The recorder — Thomas Flynn himself — had firsthand knowledge of his son's birth. This is a second original source with primary information, direct evidence, wholly independent of the baptismal register in both recorder and record type.

### Corroborating Evidence

Three additional original records independently place Patrick in Thomas Flynn's household and name Thomas as father. In the 1850 United States census for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, Patrick Flynn, age 5, appears in Thomas Flynn's household in a position consistent with a parent-child relationship (original source, indeterminate information, indirect evidence — the 1850 census did not record relationships explicitly).³ In the 1860 census for the same county, Patrick Flynn, age 15, again appears in Thomas Flynn's household, sustaining the co-residence pattern across a decade (original source, indeterminate information, indirect evidence).⁴ Patrick Flynn's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate, completed by his son-in-law James Brown, directly names Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father (original source, secondary information, direct evidence — Brown is a secondary informant for birth parentage).⁵

### Negative Evidence

A search of Schuylkill County probate records for Thomas Flynn (1870–1890) returned no will or estate administration. This absence is non-contradicting: Thomas may have died intestate, died outside the county, or left records that were not preserved.

### Resolved Conflict

The 1908 death certificate records Patrick's birthplace as Pennsylvania, while the 1850 and 1860 censuses record Ireland. The conflict was resolved in favor of Ireland: the census records are contemporaneous with Patrick's childhood and reported by a household informant likely to have known the birth facts, while the death certificate was completed 63 years after the birth by the son-in-law James Brown — a secondary informant who likely conflated Patrick's long residence in Pennsylvania with his birthplace. The 1845 Irish baptismal entry, an original record with primary information created the year of the birth, confirms County Tipperary, Ireland, as the birthplace. This conflict concerns birthplace only and does not affect the parentage conclusion.

### Conclusion

Research was conducted at FamilySearch, Ancestry, and MyHeritage for U.S. census records; FamilySearch for vital records and probate; and the National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers for the Irish baptism. A privately held family Bible was also examined. Research was declared reasonably exhaustive — no additional plausible repositories for this person, time period, and locality were identified. Two independent original sources with primary information on the parent-child relationship agree with each other and with three corroborating original sources. The single identified conflict was resolved. All five components of the Genealogical Proof Standard are satisfied: Patrick Flynn is the son of Thomas Flynn.

---

¹ St. Mary's Roman Catholic Church (County Tipperary, Ireland), baptismal register, entry for Patricius Flynn, son of Thomas Flynn and Maria Brennan, baptized 12 March 1845; National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers, microfilm 04173/04, image 287; digital image, NationalLibrary.ie, accessed 4 May 2026.

² Flynn family Bible (Holy Bible, Dublin imprint 1840), birth and family record pages, entry for Patrick Flynn, b. 1845, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan; original privately held by a Flynn descendant; digital images examined 4 May 2026.

³ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.

⁴ 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

⁵ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

fields.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1908)

The preponderance of available evidence indicates that Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) was probably the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.

### Evidence Summary

**1850 U.S. Census** (original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence of parentage). Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, is enumerated in the household of Thomas Flynn, age approximately 32, born Ireland, at dwelling 84, family 91, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.¹ The 1850 census does not record household relationships; the parent-child relationship is inferred from Patrick's position in the household, his shared surname with the head, and the age difference between them. The informant is unknown — likely a household member, possibly a neighbor — giving the information indeterminate quality. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.

**1860 U.S. Census** (original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence of parentage). Ten years later, Patrick Flynn, age 15, born Ireland, appears again in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119).² The 1860 census likewise does not state household relationships — the relationship column was not added until 1880. Patrick's continued co-residence in Thomas Flynn's household at an age consistent with the 1850 enumeration — same county, same surname, age advanced by approximately ten years — supports a parent-child relationship. This constitutes a second, independent line of indirect evidence from an original source with a different enumerator.

**1908 Death Certificate** (original source; secondary information; direct evidence of parentage). Patrick Flynn's death certificate (no. 4521, Pennsylvania Department of Health, 14 March 1908) names "Thomas Flynn" as Patrick's father.³ The informant was James Brown, identified on the certificate as Patrick's son-in-law. As a son-in-law, Brown is a secondary informant for parentage facts — he was not a witness to Patrick's birth and is reporting what he had been told. Although the informant's secondary status limits the weight of this source, the death certificate is the only record in the current evidence base that directly names the father, and it corroborates the candidate already identified by the two census co-enumerations.

The three sources are independent: the 1850 and 1860 censuses were recorded by different enumerators in different years; the death certificate was created by a separate government agency fifty-eight years after the 1850 census. Two original sources with indirect evidence and one original source with direct (secondary) evidence converge on the same candidate father.

### Conflict Resolution

A birthplace conflict exists among these three sources: the 1850 and 1860 censuses record Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate states Pennsylvania. The census records are contemporary recordings made when Patrick was a child, with informants most likely present in the household. The death certificate was created sixty-three years after Patrick's birth by a son-in-law who had no firsthand knowledge of that event. Contemporary recordings by household informants outweigh a remote secondary recollection on this point. Ireland is accepted as the birthplace; the death certificate entry is attributed to informant error — likely the son-in-law conflating Patrick's lifelong place of residence with his place of birth, or being unaware that Patrick was born in Ireland before immigrating as a young child (conflict c_001, resolved).

### Assessment

The conclusion is rated **probable** rather than **proved** for three reasons: (a) both census sources yield only indirect evidence of parentage — neither the 1850 nor the 1860 census states household relationships, so the parent-child inference rests on household position and shared surname rather than a reported fact; (b) the sole source that directly names Thomas Flynn as father (the 1908 death certificate) carries secondary information from a son-in-law informant; and (c) research remains incomplete — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not yet been searched, and several records gathered for this project have not yet been formally extracted for this question.

Thomas Flynn's 1881 will has been located (src_006). If it expressly names Patrick as a son, it would supply original, primary, direct evidence of parentage and would likely advance the conclusion to **proved**. The 1845 St. Mary's baptismal register (src_005) and the 1881 deed from Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn (src_008) are similarly positioned to strengthen the evidence base once their assertions are formally linked to this question.

### Additional Records Gathered but Not Yet Formally Extracted for This Question

The following sources are in the project record but their assertions have not been linked to q_001:

- **St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville — Baptismal Register, 1845** (src_005): Patrick Flynn's baptism. If the register names Thomas Flynn as father, this would constitute original, primary, direct evidence of parentage.
- **Thomas Flynn — Will, Schuylkill County, 1881** (src_006): Probate record for the candidate father. A bequest or naming of Patrick as "my son" would be original, primary evidence.
- **Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn — Warranty Deed, Schuylkill County, 1881** (src_008): Land conveyance from candidate father to subject; indirect but corroborating evidence of a familial relationship.
- **Patrick Flynn Obituary — Pottsville Republican, 1908** (src_009): May name surviving relatives or parents; not yet analyzed for parentage content.
- **Mary Elizabeth Flynn — Birth Certificate, 1905** (src_007): Names Patrick Flynn as father; contextual for this question only.

Formal extraction and linkage of these sources to q_001 is the recommended next step before the tier is reconsidered.

### Citations

1. 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MXYZ), accessed 1 May 2026.
2. 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MABC), accessed 2 May 2026.
3. Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 3 May 2026.

Five additional records have since been gathered but their assertions have not yet been formally linked to q_001: St. Mary's Catholic Church baptismal register 1845 (src_005), Thomas Flynn's will 1881 (src_006), Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn warranty deed 1881 (src_008), Patrick Flynn obituary in the Pottsville Republican 1908 (src_009), and Mary Elizabeth Flynn birth certificate 1905 (src_007). Probate plan item pli_006 is in progress. The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched.
fields.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1908)

The preponderance of available evidence indicates that Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) was probably the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.

### Evidence Summary

**1850 U.S. Census** (original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence of parentage). Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, is enumerated in the household of Thomas Flynn, age approximately 32, born Ireland, at dwelling 84, family 91, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.¹ The 1850 census does not record household relationships; the parent-child relationship is inferred from Patrick's position in the household, his shared surname with the head, and the age difference between them. The informant is unknown — likely a household member, possibly a neighbor — giving the information indeterminate quality. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.

**1860 U.S. Census** (original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence of parentage). Ten years later, Patrick Flynn, age 15, born Ireland, appears again in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119).² The 1860 census likewise does not state household relationships — the relationship column was not added until 1880. Patrick's continued co-residence in Thomas Flynn's household at an age consistent with the 1850 enumeration — same county, same surname, age advanced by approximately ten years — supports a parent-child relationship. This constitutes a second, independent line of indirect evidence from an original source with a different enumerator.

**1908 Death Certificate** (original source; secondary information; direct evidence of parentage). Patrick Flynn's death certificate (no. 4521, Pennsylvania Department of Health, 14 March 1908) names "Thomas Flynn" as Patrick's father.³ The informant was James Brown, identified on the certificate as Patrick's son-in-law. As a son-in-law, Brown is a secondary informant for parentage facts — he was not a witness to Patrick's birth and is reporting what he had been told. Although the informant's secondary status limits the weight of this source, the death certificate is the only record in the current evidence base that directly names the father, and it corroborates the candidate already identified by the two census co-enumerations.

The three sources are independent: the 1850 and 1860 censuses were recorded by different enumerators in different years; the death certificate was created by a separate government agency fifty-eight years after the 1850 census. Two original sources with indirect evidence and one original source with direct (secondary) evidence converge on the same candidate father.

### Conflict Resolution

A birthplace conflict exists among these three sources: the 1850 and 1860 censuses record Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate states Pennsylvania. The census records are contemporary recordings made when Patrick was a child, with informants most likely present in the household. The death certificate was created sixty-three years after Patrick's birth by a son-in-law who had no firsthand knowledge of that event. Contemporary recordings by household informants outweigh a remote secondary recollection on this point. Ireland is accepted as the birthplace; the death certificate entry is attributed to informant error — likely the son-in-law conflating Patrick's lifelong place of residence with his place of birth, or being unaware that Patrick was born in Ireland before immigrating as a young child (conflict c_001, resolved).

### Assessment

The conclusion is rated **probable** rather than **proved** for three reasons: (a) both census sources yield only indirect evidence of parentage — neither the 1850 nor the 1860 census states household relationships, so the parent-child inference rests on household position and shared surname rather than a reported fact; (b) the sole source that directly names Thomas Flynn as father (the 1908 death certificate) carries secondary information from a son-in-law informant; and (c) research remains incomplete — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not yet been searched, and several records gathered for this project have not yet been formally extracted for this question.

Thomas Flynn's 1881 will has been located (src_006). If it expressly names Patrick as a son, it would supply original, primary, direct evidence of parentage and would likely advance the conclusion to **proved**. The 1845 St. Mary's baptismal register (src_005) and the 1881 deed from Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn (src_008) are similarly positioned to strengthen the evidence base once their assertions are formally linked to this question.

### Additional Records Gathered but Not Yet Formally Extracted for This Question

The following sources are in the project record but their assertions have not been linked to q_001:

- **St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville — Baptismal Register, 1845** (src_005): Patrick Flynn's baptism. If the register names Thomas Flynn as father, this would constitute original, primary, direct evidence of parentage.
- **Thomas Flynn — Will, Schuylkill County, 1881** (src_006): Probate record for the candidate father. A bequest or naming of Patrick as "my son" would be original, primary evidence.
- **Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn — Warranty Deed, Schuylkill County, 1881** (src_008): Land conveyance from candidate father to subject; indirect but corroborating evidence of a familial relationship.
- **Patrick Flynn Obituary — Pottsville Republican, 1908** (src_009): May name surviving relatives or parents; not yet analyzed for parentage content.
- **Mary Elizabeth Flynn — Birth Certificate, 1905** (src_007): Names Patrick Flynn as father; contextual for this question only.

Formal extraction and linkage of these sources to q_001 is the recommended next step before the tier is reconsidered.

### Citations

1. 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MXYZ), accessed 1 May 2026.
2. 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MABC), accessed 2 May 2026.
3. Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 3 May 2026.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Who Were the Parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?

**Research question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn, born approximately 1845 (likely in Ireland), who died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania?

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, was the father of Patrick Flynn. No mother has been identified. This conclusion is offered at the **probable** tier; research is ongoing.

---

### Evidence

**1850 U.S. Census — Original source, indeterminate information, indirect evidence**

The 1850 federal census for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania records a five-year-old Patrick Flynn in the household of Thomas Flynn (head, approximately age 32), dwelling 84, family 91.¹ The 1850 census did not include a relationship column; the parent-child relationship is therefore inferred from household position, shared surname, and the age gap between Thomas and Patrick (approximately 27 years — consistent with a father-son relationship). The informant for names and ages was almost certainly a household member, most likely Thomas Flynn himself, but the census does not identify the informant; accordingly, information quality for those facts is classified as indeterminate. Residence is primary information, as the enumerator directly witnessed it by visiting the dwelling. This enumeration is an original source yielding indirect evidence of parentage.

**1860 U.S. Census — Original source, indeterminate information, indirect evidence**

Ten years later, the 1860 federal census for Schuylkill County records a fifteen-year-old Patrick Flynn in the same Thomas Flynn household, dwelling 112, family 119.² Patrick's reported age (15) is fully consistent with the 1850 enumeration (age 5), confirming the same individual across both censuses. As with the 1850 record, the 1860 census did not state relationships; the parent-child inference again rests on household position, shared surname, and age. The informant for age and name was most likely a household member. This enumeration is an independent original source yielding indirect evidence of continued co-residence and, by inference, a parent-child relationship.

**1908 Death Certificate — Original source, secondary information, direct evidence**

Patrick Flynn's death certificate (Pennsylvania certificate no. 4521, 12 March 1908) explicitly names Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father.³ This constitutes direct evidence of paternity. However, the informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law — a secondary informant for birth and parentage facts, reporting what he had been told rather than what he personally witnessed. A son-in-law's knowledge of his father-in-law's parentage is necessarily secondhand, recorded more than sixty years after the events it describes. The value of this source lies in its explicit naming of Thomas Flynn as father, but its secondary quality limits the independent weight it can carry.

---

### Correlation and Weighing

The three sources present a coherent and mutually reinforcing picture. Two independent original census records place Patrick Flynn continuously in the Thomas Flynn household across both 1850 and 1860, covering the entirety of Patrick's childhood and early adolescence. The death certificate, though secondary for parentage facts, explicitly confirms what the censuses imply: Thomas Flynn is the father. No source names any other candidate father; no evidence contradicts this identification.

One conflict was identified and resolved (conflict c_001): the 1850 and 1860 censuses report Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate (informant James Brown) states Pennsylvania. The census records are original sources recorded near the time of Patrick's childhood, while the death certificate reflects secondary reporting by a son-in-law more than six decades after the birth. Ireland is accepted as the correct birthplace. The death certificate error most likely reflects the son-in-law informant's confusion of Patrick's long-term place of residence with his place of birth, or his ignorance of Patrick's origins before immigration as a young child.

---

### Limitations

Research is not declared exhaustive. Probate and estate records for Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County remain unsearched (plan item pli_006, in progress). A will or estate inventory naming Patrick as a son would constitute primary information from an original source and could advance this conclusion toward proved. The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses for Patrick have not been examined; those records could further confirm or complicate the conclusion. No mother has been identified: neither the censuses nor the death certificate name Patrick's mother.

---

### Conclusion

The preponderance of available evidence **strongly suggests** that Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, was the father of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland; d. 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). Two independent original census enumerations place Patrick continuously in Thomas Flynn's household throughout his childhood, and the 1908 death certificate directly names Thomas Flynn as father. The conclusion is offered at the **probable** tier because the census relationship evidence is indirect (pre-1880 censuses did not record relationships), the death certificate informant is secondary (son-in-law), and research is not yet exhaustively declared. This conclusion should be revisited when probate records and the intervening census years are searched.

---

¹ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.
² 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.
³ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three tool calls were valid. research_append call correctly appended proof_summaries entry with all required fields: question_id q_001, tier probable, vehicle summary, all 7 supporting assertions (a_004 through a_013), and resolved conflict c_001. tree_edit correctly added ParentChild relationship with parent I2 and child I1. research_append project update completed stamping. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched live expectations.

dimensions.name: Tier justification
dimensions.rationale: The probable tier is well justified and explicitly contrasted with adjacent tiers. The narrative explains why not proved: (1) pre-1880 censuses lack relationship columns making evidence indirect, (2) death cert informant is secondary (son-in-law), (3) research not exhaustively declared with probate and intervening censuses unsearched. It explains why stronger than possible: three independent sources converge (two censuses plus death cert) with no contradicting candidates. The tier explanation directly addresses what would be needed to advance (primary documents from wills/estates, complete census search).

dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
dimensions.rationale: The narrative is fully self-contained and readable without access to research.json. It describes each evidence source in prose with inline citations (superscript footnotes 1–3), names the sources explicitly (1850 census FamilySearch, 1860 census FamilySearch, 1908 death certificate Pennsylvania State Archives), explains evidence classification (indirect vs direct, informant quality), addresses the conflict resolution (Ireland vs Pennsylvania birthplace), and declares the probable tier with rationale. A reviewer can follow the entire argument without external reference.

dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
dimensions.rationale: All relevant assertions and the resolved conflict are cited. The narrative covers a_004 and a_005 (1850 census household co-residence), a_008–a_013 (1860 census co-residence and death certificate details), and explicitly addresses c_001 (birthplace conflict). The exhaustive_search_summary field documents what has been searched and what remains (probate records, 1870/1880/1900 censuses), showing awareness of gaps. No evidence is selectively omitted or inconvenient sources ignored.

dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
dimensions.rationale: The Summary form is appropriate and correctly applied. Multiple correlated sources (two independent censuses plus death certificate) all point clearly one direction with no competing candidates or significant residual conflicts—exactly the Summary-appropriate scenario. The narrative structure follows the form: evidence section details each source, correlation section weighs them together, limitations section acknowledges gaps, conclusion restates the tier. A Summary (not Statement, not Argument) is the right choice here.

dimensions.name: Tree encoding
dimensions.rationale: The concluded tier (probable) triggered the tree-write obligation, which was fulfilled. The skill correctly invoked tree_edit to add the ParentChild relationship (parent I2 Thomas Flynn, child I1 Patrick Flynn) with relationship ID R1. The relationship links the correct persons via the correct relationship type. The conclusion now persists in both narrative (ps_001 in research.json) and structure (R1 in tree.gedcomx.json), satisfying the 'found-but-lost' guard: persons I1 and I2 are no longer unlinked.
